1: Psalms 42-43 were almost certainly a single psalm; they share the same refrain (Psalms 42:5 = Psalms 42:11 = Psalms 43:5) and Ps 43 lacks its own superscription (unusual in this section of the Psalter). Together they are the opening of Book II (Ps 42-72), the 'Elohistic Psalter' where the divine name YHWH is systematically replaced with Elohim — a different editorial practice from Book I. The 'Sons of Korah' (bene qorach) were a guild of temple singers and gatekeepers (1 Chronicles 9:17-19; Psalms 26:1-19); the Korah psalms (42-49, 84, 85, 87, 88) likely originated in their liturgical tradition. 'As a deer longs for flowing streams' — the deer panting for water in a dry wadi is the visceral image of covenant thirst.
2: 'My soul thirsts for God, for the living God. When will I come and stand in God's presence?' — the language of temple pilgrimage and desire for the divine presence. 'The living God' (el chay) as opposed to the dead gods of the nations — the one who is actually present and active. 'When will I come and appear before God' (eraeh pene Elohim?) — the phrasing echoes the pilgrimage commandment of Exodus 34:23-24: 'Three times a year all your males shall appear before the Lord GOD.' The psalmist is apparently cut off from the temple and longs for the annual festival appearances before YHWH.
3: 'My tears have been my food day and night, while people keep saying to me all day long, Where is your God?' — 'Where is your God?' (ayeh Elohecha) was the taunt of those who interpreted the psalmist's evident suffering as evidence of divine absence or abandonment (cf. Psalms 79:10; Joel 2:17; Micah 7:10). If YHWH was truly the psalmist's God and patron, why was the psalmist suffering? The taunt exploited the ANE principle that suffering = divine disfavor. Tears as 'food' — the metaphor inverts the table: instead of food that sustains, grief consumes the person.
4: 'These things I remember as I pour out my soul: how I used to walk with the crowd, leading them in procession to the house of God, with shouts of joy and thanksgiving among the festive throng' — the memory of the temple procession is both comfort and source of renewed grief. The psalmist had participated in and led (edadem, I was walking as leader) the pilgrimage processions to Jerusalem's temple — a public, joyful, communal event. Now separated from these, the memory intensifies the longing. The festive shouts and thanksgiving (qol rinnah vetodah) characterize the communal worship that the psalmist desperately misses.
5: 'Why are you downcast, O my soul? Why so disturbed within me? Put your hope in God, for I will yet praise him, my Savior and my God' — the refrain that appears in Psalms 42:5, Psalms 42:11, and 43:5 is the theological center of the combined psalm. The psalmist dialogues with his own soul (nephesh): addressing himself rather than YHWH, asking why the interior life has collapsed into despair. The self-address is therapeutic and theological: the soul's downcast state is questioned, not accepted. The affirmation 'I will yet praise him' (ki od odenu) is a future-oriented hope that refuses to interpret present circumstances as permanent.
6: 'My soul is cast down within me; therefore I remember you from the land of the Jordan, the heights of Hermon, from Mount Mizar' — the geographical markers place the psalmist in the far north of Israel: the Jordan headwaters, Mount Hermon (the highest peak in the region, on the border of modern Lebanon/Syria), and the unidentified 'little mountain' (har-Mizar). From this great distance from Jerusalem, the psalmist remembers (ezkarcha) YHWH — making memory of the covenant relationship substitute for physical presence at the temple. Distance from the sanctuary intensifies rather than destroys the covenantal connection.
7: 'Deep calls to deep at the roar of your waterfalls; all your waves and breakers have rolled over me' — the overwhelming waters image drawn from the geography of the Jordan headwaters near Hermon, where multiple streams converge with dramatic force. The 'deep calling to deep' (tehom-el-tehom qore') uses the cosmic water language of Genesis 1:2 (tehom, the primordial deep): the waters are not merely natural but cosmically threatening. YHWH's own 'waves and breakers' (mishbarecha) have swept over the psalmist — the affliction is experienced as coming from YHWH himself, not merely human enemies.
8: 'By day the LORD directs his steadfast love, and at night his song is with me, a prayer to the God of my life' — the day/night merism of continuous divine provision: hesed (covenant love/faithfulness) by day; his song (shiro, a song directed to me) by night. Night in ancient Israel was the time of greatest vulnerability (no light, predators active, enemies able to move unseen); YHWH's song accompanying the psalmist through the night is the covenant presence sustaining through maximum danger. 'God of my life' (el chayyai) — YHWH as the source and sustainer of the psalmist's vitality.
9: 'I say to God my rock, Why have you forgotten me? Why do I go about mourning under the oppression of the enemy?' — 'my rock' (sal'i, my cliff/crag) is the image of the massive, stable stone fortress that shelters those who take refuge in it (cf. Psalms 18:2; Psalms 31:3). The paradox: YHWH is named as the rock, yet the psalmist experiences 'forgetting' (shechachtani) — the divine patron apparently inactive in the crisis. The question 'Why have you forgotten me?' is the ancient form of the theodicy question: how can the covenant patron be absent when the client is in distress?
10: 'As if a sword were cutting through my bones, my enemies taunt me, saying to me all day, Where is your God?' — the taunt 'Where is your God?' returns (cf. Psalms 42:3), but here the psalmist describes it as a physical sensation: like the shattering of bones. In the honor-shame world, public taunt was not merely emotional — it was social violence. The enemies' repeated questioning of YHWH's existence or care was an attack on the psalmist's covenantal identity: to be a person whose God is absent is to be a person without standing.
11: 'Why are you downcast, O my soul? Why are you in turmoil within me? Put your hope in God; for I will yet praise him, my Savior and my God' — the second occurrence of the refrain (the third appears in Psalms 43:5). The refrain's repetition creates the structure of the combined psalm: each strophe of lament is answered by the same affirmation of future praise. This is not denial of present reality but prophetic declaration against it: despite all evidence (tears, exile, oppression, divine silence), the covenant God will be praised. The soul that is tutored by this refrain practices covenant trust in the face of its collapse.
